description: This figure contains two horizontal bar charts (a and b) reporting room-temperature compressive yield stress (in MPa, dark gray bars) and matrix Brinell hardness (in HB, light gray bars) of Fe-Al-M-C (M=Ti, V, Nb, Ta) alloys. Subplot (a) displays data for Fe-15Al-based alloys (Fe-15Al, Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-15Al-2V-1C, Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C, Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C): Ti and V-containing Fe-15Al alloys show good correlation between hardness and compressive yield stress, while Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C has higher hardness but lower yield stress than Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C. Subplot (b) displays data for Fe-26Al-based alloys (Fe-26Al, Fe-26Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-2V-1C, Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C, Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C, Fe-26Al-2Ta-1C): most Fe-26Al alloys exhibit no correlation between hardness and yield stress, and all alloys except Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C show lower compressive yield stress than binary Fe-26Al. The figure demonstrates that the hardness-yield stress relationship depends on Al content and alloying elements in this carbide-strengthened Fe-Al alloy system.

This set of figures presents room-temperature compressive engineering stress-strain curves of Fe-Al based alloys. Subfigure (a) covers 5 Fe-15Al based alloys: Fe-15Al, Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-15Al-2V-1C, Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C, Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C. Subfigure (b) covers 7 Fe-26Al based alloys: Fe-26Al, Fe-26Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-2V-1C, Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C, Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C, Fe-26Al-2Ta-1C. Black dots mark the 0.2% offset yield strength σ0.2 for each alloy. It is observed that Fe-26Al based alloys generally have higher peak strength than Fe-15Al based ones. Most alloyed Fe-Al-M-C (M=Ti, V, Nb, Ta) alloys show much stronger work hardening effects during plastic deformation compared to binary Fe-Al alloys, even though many Fe-26Al based alloyed variants have lower yield strength than binary Fe-26Al. The figure supports that alloying Fe-Al alloys with transition metals and carbon introduces carbide and Laves phase strengthening, leading to improved compressive mechanical performance at room temperature.

This figure presents room-temperature tensile engineering stress-strain curves for two series of Fe-Al-based alloys. Subplot (a) shows curves for Fe-15Al-based alloys: 1: Fe-15Al, 2: Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C, 3: Fe-15Al-2V-1C, 4: Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C, 5: Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C. Subplot (b) shows curves for Fe-26Al-based alloys: 6: Fe-26Al, 7: Fe-26Al-2Ti-1C, 8: Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C, 9: Fe-26Al-2V-1C, 10: Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C, 11: Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C, 12: Fe-26Al-2Ta-1C. Black dots mark the 0.2% offset yield strength (σ₀.₂) for each alloy, and a 2.5% engineering strain scale bar is provided. Compared to binary Fe-Al alloys, all alloys with M (Ti/V/Nb/Ta) and C additions exhibit significantly higher yield strength and ultimate tensile strength, with Fe-15Al based Nb/Ta-containing alloys reaching ~700 MPa tensile strength. The results demonstrate that MC carbide and Laves phase precipitation effectively strengthen Fe-Al alloys at room temperature.

This set of 12 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images presents room-temperature tensile fracture surfaces of Fe-Al-M-C (M = Ti, V, Nb, Ta) alloys, with a unified 10 μm scale bar for all subgraphs. Subgraph (a) corresponds to binary Fe-15Al, showing transgranular fracture with fine cleavage facets that matches its 16% room-temperature ductility. Subgraphs (b) to (e) correspond to Fe-15Al-2M-1C (M=Ti, V, Nb, Ta respectively), all exhibiting coarse transgranular cleavage consistent with their brittle behavior, where Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C has the highest ductility (~3.3%) in the Fe-15Al-based series. Subgraph (f) is binary Fe-26Al with very coarse transgranular fracture. Subgraphs (g) to (l) cover Fe-26Al-based alloys: (g) Fe-26Al-2Ti-1C, (h) Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C, (i) Fe-26Al-2V-1C, (j) Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C (highest ~1.4% ductility in Fe-26Al series with fine faceted transgranular cleavage), (k) Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C, (l) Fe-26Al-2Ta-1C. Fe-26Al alloys with Ti/Ta show mixed intergranular decohesion and transgranular cleavage, Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C fractures almost fully via intergranular decohesion, while Fe-26Al-2V-1C fracture is affected by the orientation of plate-like VC precipitates. The fracture morphologies directly correlate with alloy composition, Al content, precipitate type and room-temperature tensile ductility of the investigated alloys.

This figure presents the temperature dependency of compressive yield stress (in MPa) for five Fe-15Al-based alloys, tested from room temperature up to 800 °C. The five tested alloys are Fe-15Al (+ marker), Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C (× marker), Fe-15Al-2V-1C (○ marker), Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C (△ marker), and Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C (□ marker). All alloys show a consistent gradual, monotonic reduction in compressive yield stress as temperature increases. Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C and Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C exhibit the highest yield strength across all tested temperatures, while the binary Fe-15Al alloy has the lowest strength. These results confirm that alloying with Nb/Ta and C to form strengthening MC carbides significantly improves the compressive strength of Fe-15Al alloys at both ambient and elevated temperatures, validating the high-temperature application potential of these modified alloys.

This figure contains two subplots showing the temperature dependence of compressive yield stress for Fe-26Al based alloys from 0°C to 800°C. Subplot (a) tests alloys with Ti/V additions: binary Fe-26Al, Fe-26Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-2V-1C. All these alloys exhibit a yield stress anomaly: yield stress first decreases with increasing temperature, reaches a peak of ~400 MPa at around 550°C, then decreases again at temperatures above the peak. Subplot (b) tests alloys with Nb/Ta additions: binary Fe-26Al, Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C, Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C, Fe-26Al-2Ta-1C. The yield stress anomaly peak for Nb/Ta-containing alloys shifts to lower temperature and higher stress compared to the binary Fe-26Al alloy; Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C shows the highest strength across all tested temperatures, with its peak at 474°C and peak yield stress of 619 MPa, which is attributed to strengthening from (Fe,Al)2Nb Laves phase and MC carbides. The results demonstrate that alloying with Ti/V/Nb/Ta and C effectively improves the mechanical performance of Fe-26Al alloys for high-temperature applications.

This figure contains two horizontal bar chart subplots, showing 650 °C yield stress (unit: MPa) of Fe-Al based alloys tested under compression (dark gray bars) and tension (light gray bars). Subplot (a) displays data for Fe-15Al based alloys, including Fe-15Al, Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-15Al-2V-1C, Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C and Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C. Subplot (b) displays data for Fe-26Al based alloys, including Fe-26Al, Fe-26Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-2V-1C, Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C, Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C and Fe-26Al-2Ta-1C. All tested alloys show consistently higher compressive yield stress than tensile yield stress, confirming the tension-compression asymmetry of this alloy system at 650 °C. Nb and Ta additions deliver far more significant strengthening effect than Ti and V additions, with Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C exhibiting the highest yield stress (~440 MPa in compression, ~350 MPa in tension) among all samples. This figure verifies that carbide-forming transition metal alloying effectively improves high-temperature yield strength of Fe-Al alloys, while inherent tension-compression asymmetry remains.

This group of bar charts contains two subfigures, (a) for Fe-15Al-based alloys and (b) for Fe-26Al-based alloys, presenting the 800 °C yield stress (unit: MPa) in compression (dark gray bars) and tension (light gray bars) for Fe-Al-M-C (M=Ti, V, Nb, Ta) alloys. The Fe-15Al-based alloy series includes Fe-15Al, Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-15Al-2V-1C, Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C, Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C, while the Fe-26Al-based series covers Fe-26Al, Fe-26Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-2V-1C, Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C, Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C, Fe-26Al-2Ta-1C. All tested alloys show obvious tension-compression asymmetry, with higher compressive yield stress than tensile yield stress for every composition. Alloys with Nb or Ta addition exhibit significantly higher yield stress than those with Ti or V addition, and Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C has the highest compressive yield stress (~138 MPa) among all samples. This figure confirms the tension-compression asymmetry of the investigated Fe-Al alloy system at 800 °C, and proves that Nb and Ta are more effective for high-temperature yield strength improvement.
